characters_found.name: hadji_murad
direct_quotes: My murid.  Eldar is his name.
speech_descriptors: addressed the old man (direct question in Chechen)
actions: enters the outer door of the saklya
takes off burka, rifle, and sword and hands them to the old man
adjusts pistol at his back
comes up to the cushions and sits down wrapping his Circassian coat closer
squats beside the old man, closes his eyes, lifts hands palms upward
repeats a prayer with the old man, strokes face with both hands
asks the old man 'Ne habar?' (Is there anything new?)
nods slightly upon understanding the old man's evasion
asks no more questions after the old man's indirect answer
introduces Eldar to the old man
inner_states: understands that the old man does not wish to say what he knew and what Hadji Murad wanted to know
respects the old man's reticence — does not press further
social_markers: Chechen/Avar warlord and guest
speaks Chechen ('Ne habar', 'Habar yok')
armed (burka, rifle, sword, pistol at back) — warrior status
performs Islamic prayer ritual with host
wears Circassian coat — regional aristocratic military dress
speaks formal greeting in Islamic tradition

relationship_dynamics.quality: respectful deference to elder; trusts him as informant; does not press when evasion is clear
leader–murid bond; introduces him as 'my murid'; commands his positioning in the room
formal reciprocal greeting — proper Islamic guest etiquette

new_info: Carries pistol at his back in addition to rifle and sword
Speaks Chechen fluently (direct dialogue in Chechen dialect)
Observes Islamic prayer ritual with host — religious identity confirmed
Is actively seeking information ('Ne habar?') — on the run or in intelligence-gathering mode
Recognizes and respects indirect communication style of the old man

characters_found.name: old_man_(apiarist)
direct_quotes: I live at the apiary and have only today come to see my son....He knows.

The only news is that the hares keep discussing how to drive away the eagles, and the eagles tear first one and then another of them. The other day the Russian dogs burnt the hay in the Mitchit aoul....May their faces be torn!
speech_descriptors: replied (looking not at Hadji Murad's face but at his breast — indirect, evasive gaze)
said hoarsely and angrily (about Russians burning hay)
motioned Eldar to a place (non-verbal direction)
actions: hurries forward to open the thin, creaking door into the saklya
carefully hangs Hadji Murad's rifle and sword on a nail beside the master's weapons
squats on his bare heels beside Hadji Murad
closes his eyes, lifts hands palms upward
repeats prayer with Hadji Murad, strokes face
answers 'Habar yok' (Nothing new) while looking at HM's chest not his face
speaks in parable ('hares and eagles') to convey political news indirectly
points to Eldar and asks who he is
motions Eldar to a place on felt beside Hadji Murad
inner_states: does not wish to say directly what he knows — deliberate evasion
harbors deep anger toward the Russians (expressed hoarsely and angrily)
social_markers: elderly Chechen man (apiarist — beekeeper)
has lifeless red eyes — age/illness marker
lives apart at the apiary, visits son in the village
uses metaphorical/parabolic speech style — oral folk tradition
speaks Chechen ('Habar yok')
performs Islamic prayer
squats on bare heels — humble village elder posture
aware of local political/military events despite rural isolation

relationship_dynamics.quality: cautious host; informs indirectly via parable; avoids direct disclosure; accepts prayer ritual together — solidarity
neutral assessment ('That is well'); directs him to a seat — authoritative host role
lives apart from son; son knows the news — defers knowledge to son

new_info: He is the father of the host (Sado's father or the master of the house's father — referred to as 'my son')
Has lifeless red eyes — distinctive physical marker
Uses fable/parable as political speech ('hares and eagles' = Chechens and Russians)
References concrete recent atrocity: Russians burned hay in the Mitchit aoul
Deliberately avoids direct disclosure of intelligence to Hadji Murad — protective caution or fear of surveillance
